sound_type: resonant machine-gun drum on dead snag -- deep booming concussive burst, 15-16 strikes/sec, 40-80 Hz fundamental, loudest drum of any North American woodpecker, carries 1+ km through forest, E=6 MAXIMUM
ecosystem: Dead snag in mature/old-growth forest -- Douglas-fir, western red cedar, Sitka spruce. Hollow dead trees as resonating chambers. Large-diameter snags for lowest frequency drums.
season: Year-round resident. Does not migrate. Drums most intensely in late winter through spring (breeding season) but drums year-round for territory maintenance.

coordinates.description: Reference for old-growth forest habitat in the central Cascades. Pileated Woodpeckers drum on large dead snags in mature and old-growth forest throughout the PNW.

taxonomy_note.description: SECOND CONSECUTIVE PILEATED -- the only species with two consecutive entries. Degree 108 was the wild laughing call (vocal). Degree 109 is the drumming (mechanical). Third consecutive Picidae (107-108-109). Third entry total for Dryocopus pileatus (54, 108, 109). E=6 on both entries (108, 109).
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Picidae count: 6 entries (Northern Flicker 21-22, Red-breasted Sapsucker 54, Pileated Woodpecker 54, Acorn Woodpecker 107, Pileated Woodpecker laughing 108, Pileated Woodpecker drumming 109)
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Dryocopus from Greek drys ('tree') + kopos ('beating') -- 'tree-beater.' The etymology is literally the drumming action: the tree-beater beats trees. pileatus from Latin pileatus ('capped, crested').
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Dryocopus -- third entry. The genus name 'tree-beater' is most directly relevant to degree 109 (drumming) -- this IS tree-beating.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Picidae continues for a third consecutive degree. Three Picidae in three degrees. Tracking toward the Sciuridae record (8 consecutive).

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 40-80 Hz. The drum is EXTREMELY low-frequency. The large bill and resonant dead snag produce deep bass tones that propagate efficiently through forest.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 80-400 Hz. Most acoustic energy concentrated in the low-frequency range. Below the primary frequency range of most songbirds -- minimal acoustic competition.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Drum: 40-80 Hz fundamental with harmonics to 2,000+ Hz. The resonant snag produces a rich harmonic series.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Drum roll: 1-3 seconds. A rapid burst of powerful strikes. The most powerful rolls sustain for the full 3 seconds.
song_metrics.note_structure: MACHINE-GUN DRUM: Rapid burst of evenly spaced bill strikes on dead snag. 15-16 strikes per second. Mechanical regularity. Each strike is a distinct concussive impact. Begins rapidly, maintains peak speed, decelerates slightly.
song_metrics.note_types: Drumming (primary, territorial/mate attraction), wild laughing call (degree 108, territorial/contact), softer contact calls. Degree 109 focuses exclusively on the drumming.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Single burst. Each drum roll is one continuous rapid sequence. No repeated phrases. One explosive burst per drumming event, though multiple events may occur in succession.
song_metrics.timbre: Deep. Booming. Resonant. Concussive. The timbre is shaped by the resonating dead snag -- hollow interiors produce richer, lower-frequency resonance. The sound is more like a bass drum than a snare. Machine-like regularity.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=6 -- MAXIMUM ENERGY. The loudest drum of any North American woodpecker. 80-100+ dB at 1 meter. Carries 1+ km through forest habitat. The low-frequency energy propagates efficiently with less atmospheric absorption than higher frequencies.
song_metrics.tempo: 15-16 strikes per second. Slower than smaller woodpeckers (Hairy: ~25/sec, Flicker: 25-30/sec) but with vastly more force per strike. The power compensates for the lower rate.
song_metrics.context: TERRITORIAL AND REPRODUCTIVE. Drums to advertise territory boundaries, attract mates, and maintain pair bonds. Most intense in late winter through spring but occurs year-round. Males and females both drum. The drum announces: this snag, this territory, this forest is occupied.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal variation in drum characteristics across range. The drum tree's resonance properties have more effect on sound quality than regional variation.
song_metrics.key_property: MACHINE-GUN DRUM ON DEAD SNAG -- the percussive maximum. E=6 MAXIMUM. 15-16 strikes/sec, 40-80 Hz fundamental, 1-3 sec duration, carries 1+ km. Loudest drum of any North American woodpecker. Dead snag as resonating instrument. SECOND CONSECUTIVE PILEATED (108 laughing, 109 drumming). ONLY SPECIES WITH TWO CONSECUTIVE ENTRIES. Two independent sound systems both at E=6. THIRD CONSECUTIVE PICIDAE (107-108-109). 49th consecutive original CSV.

plumage.overall: Identical to degree 108 -- same species. Large, crow-sized woodpecker. Predominantly black with bright red crest. White wing linings. White facial stripe.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: For degree 109: the DRUMMING POSTURE is diagnostic. The Pileated braces against a large dead snag, bill drawn back, then delivers rapid powerful strikes. The bird's entire body participates in the drumming motion -- not just the head and neck.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Minimal. Year-round plumage. Same as degree 108.
plumage.flight_pattern: Deep undulating flight between drum trees. Same as degree 108.

physical_description.overall: Same as degree 108. Large woodpecker. Body length 40-49 cm. Wingspan 66-75 cm. Weight 250-400 g. The size is directly relevant to drumming -- larger body = more mass behind each strike = more force = louder drum.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 109: the BILL is the primary instrument. The Pileated has the largest, most chisel-shaped bill of any North American woodpecker. Each drumming strike delivers approximately 1,000-1,200 g of deceleration.
physical_description.field_id: Very large black woodpecker with red crest drumming on a large dead snag. The deep, booming, machine-gun drum is unmistakable. If you hear a powerful, resonant, rapid drumming on a large snag in old-growth forest -- it is a Pileated Woodpecker.

nodes.title: The Percussive Maximum -- Loudest Drum in North America
nodes.description: 15-16 strikes/sec on resonant dead snag. 40-80 Hz fundamental. 1-3 sec duration. Carries 1+ km. E=6 MAXIMUM. The loudest drum of any North American woodpecker. Machine-gun percussion.

nodes.title: Two Sound Systems Both at E=6 -- Voice (108) + Percussion (109)
nodes.description: Only species with two consecutive entries. Wild laughing call (syrinx, 800-3,500 Hz, E=6) and drumming (bill strikes, 40-80 Hz, E=6). Two independent mechanisms. Frequency dominance from sub-bass to upper midrange.

nodes.title: The Dead Snag as Resonating Instrument
nodes.description: Hollow interior amplifies. Large diameter lowers frequency. Elevated position radiates. The bird selects and returns to specific drum trees. The dead snag is a percussion instrument played by the Pileated.

nodes.title: Second Consecutive Pileated -- Only Species with Two Consecutive Entries
nodes.description: Degree 108 (laughing call) and degree 109 (drumming). Same species, different sound-production mechanisms. Both E=6. Third consecutive Picidae (107-108-109).

nodes.title: Keystone Cavity Creator -- Dead Snag Economy
nodes.description: The Pileated excavates the largest cavities of any NA woodpecker (20 cm wide, 60 cm deep). Dozens of secondary species use these cavities. The drum declares territory; the excavations build community infrastructure.

cross_references.relationship: CONSECUTIVE PILEATED! Only species with two consecutive entries. Degree 108: wild laughing wuk-wuk-wuk (vocal, syrinx, 800-3,500 Hz, E=6). Degree 109: drumming (mechanical, bill strikes, 40-80 Hz, E=6). Two sound systems, both at maximum.
THIRD PILEATED ENTRY. Degree 54 (general, E=5), degree 108 (laughing, E=6), degree 109 (drumming, E=6). Most entries for any species. 55-degree gap from original.
THIRD CONSECUTIVE PICIDAE (107-108-109). Acorn Woodpecker started the streak. Three woodpecker species, three dramatically different sounds. Tracking toward Sciuridae record (8).
PICIDAE DRUM COMPARISON. Flicker drums at 25-30/sec, moderate-loud. Pileated drums at 15-16/sec, LOUDEST. Same family, different strategies: rapid and light vs. slow and devastating.
CAVITY ECONOMY. The Pileated's drumming advertises territory; its excavations build cavities. The Flying Squirrel uses these cavities. Drum = territorial advertisement for the cavity economy.
